model_output_raw_text: {"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 64","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The verifier budget is fixed at 256 optimizer steps, so the current run only sees about 16k training examples. Doubling the batch size lets the model consume roughly twice as many examples within the same step budget while keeping the rest of the optimization setup unchanged.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"With a fixed 256-step budget, increasing the batch again should let the model cover roughly a full epoch of CIFAR-10 instead of about two-thirds of one. The current optimizer and schedule are already stable, so this is a simple way to trade step count for more data exposure.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The move to batch size 256 substantially reduced gradient noise and increased data coverage, so the optimizer can likely handle a somewhat larger peak learning rate. A moderate increase keeps the current schedule intact while testing whether faster early progress lowers loss further within the same 256-step budget.","secondary_modes":["micro","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"OPTIMIZER = \"adamw\"","old":"OPTIMIZER = \"adam\"","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"The current setup already has a useful learning-rate schedule and substantial batch size, so switching from coupled Adam weight decay to decoupled AdamW is a clean optimizer-family test. This often improves generalization for MLPs without forcing broader hyperparameter changes.","secondary_modes":["caching","topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.1)","old":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The model now trains for roughly a full epoch with a fairly aggressive optimizer setup, so a small amount of label smoothing is a simple regularization test that can reduce overconfident logits and improve validation loss without changing the training loop.","secondary_modes":["indexing"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()","old":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.1)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"Label smoothing hurt validation loss in this budgeted setting, so the next useful step is to remove it and return to the stronger baseline before testing other ideas. This keeps the optimizer and schedule that were working while undoing the regressive regularization change.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DEPTH = 3","old":"DEPTH = 2","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The optimization setup now looks reasonably tuned, so the next likely bottleneck is model expressiveness. Adding one more hidden block is a simple capacity increase that preserves the same training loop and normalization pattern while testing whether extra depth helps within the roughly one-epoch fixed-step budget.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 14","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 12","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"Depth helped, which suggests the model is still capacity-limited. A modest width increase keeps the same architecture pattern and optimizer settings while testing whether more hidden units improve the deeper MLP without a large complexity jump.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 384","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The deeper and wider MLP is still training under a fixed 256-step budget, so increasing batch size is a direct way to expose it to more of the dataset without altering the optimizer logic. A moderate jump to 384 is a safer continuation of the earlier batch-size gains than a more aggressive doubling.","secondary_modes":["layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 384","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The strongest gains so far have come from letting the model cover more of the dataset within the fixed 256-step budget. {"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 768","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The strongest consistent improvements have come from increasing dataset coverage under the fixed 256-step budget. {"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 1024","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 768","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Batch-size scaling continues to be the clearest source of gains, which suggests the fixed-step regime still benefits from seeing more examples rather than taking noisier updates. {"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"With batch size now at 1024, gradient noise is much lower and the model is effectively training for several epochs within the fixed-step budget.
